summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to a high school student who doubts their mathematical ability because they're slower than peers. She argues that speed is not what makes a good mathematician—it's a 'style' difference, not a shortcoming. Math competitions create a false impression that quickness equals talent, but real mathematical work rewards patience and deep thinking. She encourages the student to ignore competitive pressures, take challenging courses like Galois Theory, and recognize that math is fundamentally a collaborative, patient discipline that welcomes those who love it.
key_points: Speed in mathematics is a style difference, not a measure of ability or potential
Math competitions create harmful false impressions about what it means to be 'good at math'
Being good at math is mostly about how much you want to spend your time doing it
As math gets more advanced and 'awesomer,' the pace naturally slows down
Mathematics is a collaborative, crowd-sourced endeavor despite media narratives about competitive geniuses
money_quotes: There's always someone faster than you.
But it's not a race. Mathematics is patient and doesn't mind.
Think of it, your slowness, or lack of quickness, as a style thing but not as a shortcoming.
In reality, mostly of being good at math is really about how much you want to spend your time doing math.
As the math gets awesomer, the speed gets slower.
Believe me, as you know more you will realize more and more how little you know.
Math is not a competitive sport. It's one of the only existing truly crowd-sourced projects of society.
It's been around a long time and is patient to be added to by you when you have the love and time and will to do so.
key_insight: Mathematical ability is not about speed but about sustained curiosity and willingness to spend time thinking deeply—math is patient and collaborative, not a competitive race.
